text: This video demonstrates early transanal closure after vacuum assisted drainage for anastomatic leakage in rectal cancer surgery. This case involves a seventy-seven year old male patient presenting with a T-three-N-zero rectal tumor with a clear circumferential resection margin. Patient did not receive neoadjuvant treatment and underwent a low anterior resection with a side-to-end anastomosis, six centimeters from the anal verge. Patient did not receive a diverting ileostomy.

On postoperative day four, the patient developed increasing abdominal pain with high infectious parameters. Abdominal CT scan showed an anastomatic leak from the circular anastomosis. The patient was taken to the operating theater for a relaparoscopy and creation of a diverting ileostomy. Endosponge treatment was started to control pelvic sepsis.
Endosponges were changed every three to four days. Usually it only takes two to four endosponge changes to obtain a clean cavity, but this case took particularly long.

Two months post-operation, the presacral cavity is clean with visible granulation tissue, ready for closure. Under general anesthesia, the transanal platform is installed, revealing the blind loop, afferent loop, and anastomotic defect. Gauze is inserted into the afferent loop to prevent accidental closure during suturing. The cavity shows clean granulation tissue, but increased fibrosis necessitates mobilization of the rectal cuff from surrounding structures.

The surgeon inserts a six to eight French suction drain into the old abscess cavity, positioning it one to two centimeters distal to the anastomotic defect, immediately posterior to the rectal wall. The drain is then secured with a fast-resorbable Ficryl-III-Zero suture to prevent the formation of a new abscess.

The transanal platform is removed and repositioned to keep the suction drain outside its lumen. The drain is visibly fixated outside the platform. The surgeon closes the cavity using separate interrupted full-thickness Ficryl-II-Zero sutures. Gas is observed in the afferent loop, preventing its closure during the suturing process. After the defect is fully closed, the gas will be removed to confirm the afferent loop's patency.

Standard postoperative protocol entails drain removal on postoperative day five to seven and oral antibiotics for seven to ten days. Anastomotic integrity is checked by an endoscopy and a CT scan with rectal contrast. If both show a healed anastomosis, the diverting ileostomy can be closed.
This video demonstrates our technique for approximation of the anastomotic defect after vacuum assisted drainage. Thank you for watching.
